summary:
The Red Pyramid at Dahshur, built by Pharaoh Sneferu around 2590–2570 BC, was the world's first successfully completed true smooth-sided pyramid, marking a decisive breakthrough in ancient Egyptian monumental architecture. Named for the reddish hue of its limestone core stones, it rises at a consistent angle of approximately 43 degrees and stands about 105 meters tall, making it the third-largest ancient Egyptian pyramid. Its construction followed the experimental Bent Pyramid at the same site and incorporated the lessons of Sneferu's earlier building campaigns.

The Red Pyramid served as Sneferu's actual burial monument, and it is generally accepted that he was interred within its internal chambers. The structural success of the Red Pyramid directly enabled his son Khufu to commission the [[event:1708|Great Pyramid of Giza]], which surpassed it in scale but followed the same fundamental design principles. The Red Pyramid thus occupies a pivotal position in the history of architecture: it represents the culmination of nearly a century of Egyptian pyramid-building experimentation and the starting point for the classical pyramid tradition that reached its apex at Giza.
